topic: How the Electoral College Actually Works
key_narrative: The Electoral College isn't a direct popular vote — it's a state-by-state contest where 538 electors determine the presidency, and the system's quirks mean a candidate can win the presidency while losing the national popular vote.
source_summary: Sources include National Archives official data, Pew Research Center analysis, and academic papers. Overall reliability: HIGH. Multiple primary sources cross-verified.

facts.claim: The Electoral College has 538 electors — one for each member of Congress plus three for Washington D.C.
facts.evidence: Article II, Section 1 of the Constitution, as amended by the 23rd Amendment.

facts.claim: A candidate needs 270 electoral votes to win — a simple majority of the 538 total.
facts.evidence: This threshold comes from half of 538 (269) plus one. It's not in the Constitution explicitly but is a mathematical consequence of the total.

facts.claim: Five times in U.S. history, the winner of the Electoral College lost the national popular vote — most recently in 2000 and 2016.
facts.evidence: 1824, 1876, 1888, 2000, 2016. In 2016, Hillary Clinton won the popular vote by nearly 3 million but lost the Electoral College 304-227.

facts.claim: Most states use a winner-take-all system — the candidate who wins the state's popular vote gets ALL of that state's electoral votes. Only Maine and Nebraska split their votes by congressional district.
facts.evidence: 48 states + DC use winner-take-all. Maine (since 1972) and Nebraska (since 1992) use the congressional district method.

facts.claim: Small states have disproportionately more electoral power per voter. A Wyoming resident's vote carries about 3.6 times the weight of a California resident's vote in the Electoral College.
facts.evidence: Wyoming has 3 electoral votes for ~580K people (1 elector per 193K). California has 54 electoral votes for ~39M people (1 elector per 722K).

facts.claim: If no candidate reaches 270 electoral votes, the House of Representatives chooses the president — with each state delegation getting ONE vote.
facts.evidence: 12th Amendment to the Constitution. This has happened twice: 1800 and 1824.

facts.claim: The Electoral College was originally designed as a compromise between letting Congress pick the president and a direct popular election, and it was also influenced by the Three-Fifths Compromise.
facts.evidence: At the Constitutional Convention of 1787, Southern states wanted slaves counted for representation (but not taxation), which gave them more electoral votes without extending voting rights.

facts.claim: Over 700 proposals have been introduced in Congress to reform or abolish the Electoral College — more than any other constitutional issue.
facts.evidence: Congressional Research Service records. The most popular alternative is the National Popular Vote Interstate Compact.

visual_leads.suggested_crop: Full map with state labels highlighted

visual_leads.description: US population density map for electoral power comparison
visual_leads.suggested_crop: Highlight CA vs WY population contrast

contested_flags.claim: The Electoral College protects small states from being ignored in presidential campaigns
contested_flags.counter_claim: In practice, candidates spend almost all their time in ~10 swing states, ignoring both small safe states and large safe states equally.
contested_flags.resolution: Both perspectives have merit. Small-state protection was the design intent, but swing-state concentration is the observed outcome.

contested_flags.claim: Without the Electoral College, candidates would only campaign in big cities
contested_flags.counter_claim: The current system concentrates campaigning in swing states. A national popular vote would make every vote in every state equally valuable.
contested_flags.resolution: Debated outcome. The National Popular Vote Interstate Compact represents one proposed solution.
